description: Length-balanced version of LLM hallucination facts. Target ratio: 0.8-1.3

facts.context: LLM hallucination refers to model outputs that are:
facts.truth: fluent but factually incorrect or fabricated
distractors: always detectable by the model itself automatically
only occurring in smaller models under 10B params
eliminated by scaling up model size to 100B plus

facts.context: Closed-book QA (no retrieval) hallucination rates are problematic because:
facts.truth: models confabulate plausible but false details
distractors: models refuse to answer without retrieval access
accuracy exceeds human performance consistently
hallucination only affects open-book settings

facts.context: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) reduces but does not eliminate hallucination because:
facts.truth: models may ignore or hallucinate beyond context
distractors: RAG guarantees factual accuracy in all cases
retrieval is always perfect and comprehensive
hallucination is impossible with external data

facts.context: LLM-generated citations to academic papers are unreliable because:
facts.truth: fake papers, authors, and DOIs are fabricated
distractors: models only cite real papers from training set
citation accuracy exceeds 95 percent verified
fake citations are self-detected by models

facts.context: LLM confidence calibration is problematic because:
facts.truth: high confidence even when factually wrong
distractors: models are well-calibrated by default always
low confidence indicates all errors reliably
calibration improves with scale automatically

facts.context: Teaching LLMs to say 'I don't know' is difficult because:
facts.truth: training rewards plausible over honest answers
distractors: models naturally refuse uncertain queries always
RLHF fully solves this uncertainty problem now
uncertainty is always expressed very accurately

facts.context: Entity hallucination (confusing similar entities) occurs because:
facts.truth: models blend attributes of similar entities
distractors: entities stored in separate memory banks always
confusion only happens with fictional entities
larger models never confuse entities at all

facts.context: Temporal hallucination in LLMs refers to:
facts.truth: mixing up dates and temporal ordering errors
distractors: only affects events after training cutoff date
is fully solved by adding timestamp metadata
temporal reasoning is a core LLM strength

facts.context: Self-consistency decoding (sampling multiple times and voting) helps with:
facts.truth: reducing variance but not systematic errors
distractors: guaranteeing correct answers in all cases
eliminating all factual errors completely
only working for math problems specifically

facts.context: Attribution and source grounding in LLMs is challenging because:
distractors: each fact links to its training source text
attribution is solved by attention analysis
models store explicit source metadata always

facts.context: Factual recall in LLMs degrades for:
facts.truth: long-tail entities and rare training facts
distractors: common knowledge more than rare facts seen
all facts equally regardless of frequency
no correlation with training frequency at all

facts.context: Automated hallucination detection is difficult because:
facts.truth: requires external verification that may be incomplete
distractors: model logits reliably indicate hallucination risk
hallucinations have distinctive detectable patterns
detection is already a fully solved problem
